questions.prompt: A branch encoding is PC-relative and instruction addresses are measured in bytes. What must the assembler use for a label operand?

choices.B: The label byte address minus the branch instruction's defined PC base
choices.C: The absolute label address with no range check

questions.explanation: PC-relative encoding uses an address difference from the ISA-defined PC base. Source line counts do not account for instruction width, directives, or layout.

questions.prompt: If an instruction faults before its architectural operation completes, what is the safest teaching-CPU commit rule?

choices.A: Commit whichever register and memory writes happened first
choices.B: Commit no instruction-visible writes and report the precise fault state
choices.C: Advance the PC twice and continue

questions.explanation: A precise fault leaves the instruction's architectural writes uncommitted and records where and why it failed. Partial writes make restart and differential traces ambiguous.

questions.prompt: A differential CPU trace first differs in the destination register after an ADD instruction while PC and source registers still agree. Which component is the best first suspect?
questions.explanation: The first differing architectural write occurs at ADD, so its decoded operation, arithmetic, or destination write is the narrowest causal boundary. Later failures are downstream.

questions.prompt: Which boot-monitor validation order is safest before copying a payload?

choices.B: Read declared length, allocate without limits, then check destination
choices.C: Validate header and arithmetic, validate destination range, receive bounded bytes, verify integrity, then permit jump
choices.D: Accept any address if the serial link is local

questions.explanation: Structure, arithmetic, and range must be proven before writes; completeness and integrity must be proven before control transfer. A local transport is not authorization.

questions.prompt: Why should an emulator check instruction alignment before fetching bytes?

choices.B: Because misaligned fetch behavior is part of the architecture and unchecked fetch can read the wrong or out-of-range bytes
choices.C: Because all data accesses are always aligned
choices.D: Only to improve host compiler optimization

questions.explanation: The ISA defines legal instruction alignment and fault behavior. Checking before memory access preserves precise traps and bounds.
